note:
Meta-Note：很抱歉，文章之间已经这么长时间了；无论是好是坏，我都会在这里做些有趣的事情，有时工作和家庭会给我带来麻烦。在这种情况下，我花了大量时间尝试学习SQL。您会很高兴地注意到，我现在可以用他们中的最好的技能来补充“中级数据库语言技能”。
 几年前，我的妻子在一个朋友的共同认识下主持婴儿洗礼。在与女主人的交谈中，我的妻子得知他们的财务状况很艰难-他们总是破产，而且没有多少预算似乎可以帮助他们取得成功。他们削减了所有可能的成本，事情变得越来越糟。她向我的妻子承认，她只是觉得自己正在下沉到越来越深的水下，并且没有发现她或她的家人的出路。
 注意：女主人和她的丈夫都是医生。他们每年的总收入超过20万美元，随着谈话的进行，我的妻子了解到他们的问题开始并停止了，女主人无法省下足够多的钱，而这笔钱要花在他们非常漂亮的房子上并制造了当年的汽车。当时她靠我的妻子寻求财务方面的情感支持时，我们一家四口的年收入不足30,000美元。
 您应该知道，女主人至少一点都不卑鄙，我们那时才喜欢她，并继续这样做。但是她确实有一种隧道视野，此后我注意到这种现象越来越普遍：如果您来自一个财务状况良好的家庭，上过大学，然后立即开始自己做得很好，那么很难获得任何一种低收入水平生活的背景。这不是相对富裕的人愚蠢或麻木不仁的问题；很难理解从未有过的生活，这是完全合法的，而且经常会为为什么有人选择少赚钱而不是通过培训和教育来提高自己的收入而感到困惑。
 本着这种精神，我想提供我的服务，作为一种差强人意的指南，以帮助您了解铁轨另一侧的情况。在这个职位上，我有两种资格。首先是常见的-我们从未在财务上做得特别出色。近年来，随着我终于转向低层阶级的上流社会，情况有所好转，但Covid提醒我们，这种合格的成功是多么短暂。我们不得不以许多有趣的方式来节省开支，在这里和那里做出艰难的选择，有时（通常）完全没有选择。这并不容易。
 同时，我大部分时候都很开心。我有一个很棒的妻子，她对附近的生活感到非常满意，有两个孩子会尽可能地适应我的个性，还有数十个在线和离线的朋友会为我做子弹，反之亦然。这很重要，因为我想让您知道我并不想让您感到难过-我知道并了解每个人都有不同的问题，而且我在生活在不完美世界中也不是唯一。我不是要夸大政治观点或试图通过立法。不要对本文感到内--这只是提供信息，而不是发出武装呼吁。
    在与处于或高于生活工资水平的人们交谈时，我遇到的最大障碍之一是通常认为事物的质量与价格呈线性关系。从“一切顺利”一直到“杰夫·贝佐斯”，在大多数价格点上都是如此-如果您多付一点钱，您就会得到更多。如果您少付一点钱，您就会少付一点钱。 
因为这种关系是您所期望的，所以您会得到很多听起来很合理的建议，尽管这不是完全错误的，但却不能很好地抓住穷人价格机制的细微差别。根据这则随机选择的文章，我真的应该确保不要将我收入的30％用于租金：
  尽管每个人的情况都是独特的，但许多专家表示，最好将每月总收入的不超过30％的支出用于住房相关的支出，包括房租和水电费。根据该规定，最好确保您在房租上花费的金额远低于家庭收入的30％。换句话说，如果您每个月的收入为$ 3,000，建议您支付不超过$ 900的房租和其他住房费用。
  这里的普遍想法是，如果我的支出超过30％，就会使自己在预算的所有其他方面都变得脆弱；当我的工作地点可能会变得更糟并且与财务状况更加接近时，为什么还要负担更多的负担呢？从每年5万以上的人群的角度来看，这是有道理的-毕竟，1250美元将在许多市场上租出合理的房屋或公寓。在大城市凤凰城，这可能看起来像这样：
    而且这非常适合居住-它很小（认为大于900平方英尺），但是否则，它是一个保存良好的地方。这不完全是凡尔赛宫，但您不一定会像怪物一样通过在这里租房来告诉某人以自己的能力生活。但是，当您与时薪为17.50美元的某人打交道时，会发生什么呢？每月840能给您带来什么？
    我不是100％确定这是改建的Motel 6，但是我不是100％确定不是。当我向您展示的第一套公寓位于城镇中一个相对宜人，宜居的地方时，该公寓位于较易开火的区域，当地人不称其为“三角形”。凤凰城主要采用砾石和混凝土美化环境，可以很好地进行清理（请注意，最近仍在潮湿的软管混凝土），但请不要误解：这不是由糖尿病患者完全拥有皮下注射针头的那种公寓大楼。
 这就是您在较低价格水平上遇到的麻烦-“这是一间小巧但可以接受的公寓”和“位于Stab-ville的贫民窟公寓”之间没有任何关系。
 所有这些都不是完全打消上面的预算建议-如果我做出完美的预算决定，这绝对是我现在应该住在的那种公寓。但是请考虑一下，这是我必须做出的选择-我是否要在公寓上多花50％的预算，把预算压死，还是让我的妻子和孩子们在我家15英尺内有5个前门的地方镇上最凶的部分？两种选择都不是很好，但是缺乏中间选择会迫使您选择其中一项。 
无论情况如何，收入中等的人都必须准备长期坚持租金。这是因为这两个级别的公寓都非常准确地假定您负担不起律师的费用-通常放下一两个月的租金作为保证金是正常的，但取回这笔钱要少得多。公寓大楼没有理由退还他们可以简单保留的数千美元。这意味着您每次搬家时，都需要为该特权支付一个月工资的三分之一左右的费用。由于违反租约通常意味着您失去了在非荒芜环境中生活的特权，这意味着如果您在一年中的正确时间没有现金储备（这些以后会有更多*），您可能会住在同一地方不管您是否喜欢，又可以整整一年。
 注意：有人告诉我，我完全忘记谈论现金储备及其对这种情况的影响。还需要指出的是，这些数字仅在特定市场“有意义”，即在其他地方居住的价格便宜或昂贵，因此此处的确切数字并非对每个人都适用。我会写一篇后续文章，以补充一些此类反馈。
    生活在体面到伟大的收入水平的很少有人害怕自己的车，我总是感到震惊。似乎他们几乎没有在晚上醒着，甚至根本没有想过他们那飘忽不定的交流发电机。斯科特·亚历山大（Scott Alexander）在解释正面体验方面做得非常出色：
  当我刚开始与贫困患者一起工作时，我震惊于他们生活中有多少与汽车有关的问题。对于像我这样的小康人来说，有车就是背景噪音。您以合理的价格购买或租借它，然后再也不用担心它。贫穷的人买不起，而且总是没有足够的信誉来出租。它们往往会变得更老，更粗略，并且会经常发生故障。我经常听到这样的抱怨：“我的车坏了，我付不起维修费用，如果我不能胜任工作，我将被解雇。”他们中有些人买不起保险，没有保险就抓住机会。其他人则与警察发生了各种事件，使他们失去了执照，但他们不能只是不露面，所以他们还是开车，希望不要被捕。
  这实际上与体验非常接近。这表明斯科特（Scott）认为租约的问题仅来自信贷问题-每月300至400辆漂亮的底桶福特租赁。对于一个年收入30,000-40000k的人来说，这大约是他们收入的10-15％，在我们谈论保险之前；再加上您无法在大多数租约中购买仅责任保险，我们已经处于昂贵的价格范围内。
 但是还有其他事情-如上所述，责任保险通常是您唯一有意义的选择-如果您的汽车要价$ 2000，那么每月为高扣除额额外支付$ 50-100的费用就不会加起来；如果您需要使用自付额，您仍然有$ 1000的自付额。但是，由于您一开始买不起，所以您无需考虑这一点；您只要时刻疯狂地小心翼翼地开始驾驶，并祈祷遇到的任何事故都不是您的错。一滑而过，您要么根本无法上班，要么花费更多时间。请记住：这里不是Uber的选择；每月约400美元的费用会使您破产。
 您也或多或少地被迫学习做机械工作。我是行业管理员-我通常从事附加了“助理”的工作。我最大的骄傲之一就是打字速度，当人们问我的爱好时，我告诉他们“ Excel”。如果您以我的兴趣和天生的技能来判断我，那么您将不会期望我能够更换自己的轮胎。但是多年来，我的工作量使我不得不变得相当体面-我已经完成了离合器的翻新和顶垫的工作，以及其他一些我本可以避免的痛苦的工作（对我而言）。但这通常是我唯一的选择-它要么想出如何更换交流发电机，要么在周末进行全面制动，或者失去我不到一小时即可到达杂货店的能力。请记住，这里节省的钱只是人工-零件仍然有不可避免的成本。 
实际的结果是，每当我开车时，它就会非常缓慢，非常谨慎，并在恐惧中聆听汽车发出的任何细微噪音。这是一个持续不断的压力，它是有限的；我们不经常去长途旅行，当我们和妻子开玩笑时，通常会说“嗯，如果汽车在其他地方抛锚了，我们就会开始住在那儿”。
 合理地问“如果汽车太可怕了，为什么不使用公共交通工具？”。我住在一个城市，实际上，这是一个可以解决我许多问题的选择：我不必担心维修，我的汽油和保险账单会消失，公交车上发生的任何事情都是城市的问题，不是我的。特别是在紧急情况下，公共交通将是天赐之物，并且可能是保持和失去工作之间的区别。
 但与此同时，还需要考虑其他事项。我的通勤时间不短，而且对巴士不友好；从单程大约30分钟到往返不到4小时。而当您的通勤在一个糟糕的地方开始和结束时（这是低收入家庭从事低薪工作的典型情况），这意味着每天要花四个小时，周围的人充其量从正常的通勤者到精神上明显的人群失衡，并试图在整个旅程中与您交谈。通过与“我们的大众运输很棒！”中的朋友交谈，我了解到并非每个地方都有这种情况，但您变得越穷，这通常会使情况变得更糟。我可以通过做一个更糟糕的工作来缩短通勤时间（也许-就业市场目前还不是很好），但这本身就限制了职业发展。
    当我试图向儿子们解释公司如何决定向某人付钱时，通常会发生以下情况：公司希望向某人支付尽可能少的薪水并保留该人，因此，某人的薪资取决于他们的技能多么稀有，对这些技能有多少需求。他们的工作价值并没有那么重要-行政助理可能会每天接触公司的每个部门并协助开展大量工作，但他们的报酬仍然不高-很难证明何时可以雇用和培训一个人几乎可以轻松完成几乎相同的事情。
 我认为这是一种看待薪酬的相当准确的方法，但它适用于工作的其他方面。如果您今年生病的次数比平常多，并且用完了法定的病假，您的公司会因您再次生病而将您开除，还是要求您请病假？如果公司决定减薪，您的职位是公司没有花费大量金钱和时间来填补的廉价工作吗？如果您没有稀有技能，则管理层会知道他们可以要求您加班，避免让您利用带薪休假并一时兴起更改工作职责清单-他们和您都知道如果您获胜，不这样做，他们就能找到明天的人。并非每家公司在这些方面都是不好的-优秀的人才的确存在-但是每家公司都知道他们可以做到，并且无论他们打算做什么，这都会使工作的各个方面变得色彩斑color。
 尽管您的工作价值低，可替代性低，但您倾向于获得的最差待遇可能是违反直觉的，这可能接近生活工资。这些工作趋向于需要一个可以花大量时间进行需要单点联系的客户交互的人。在不愉快的情况下，他们会奖励长时间的集中注意力。换句话说，这些都是高压力的工作，有很多强制性的加班和非常快的节奏-他们付出了很多，因为他们无法让别人去做。尽管有薪水，这些工作的流失还是荒谬的-有些人可以跟上他们的工资，但这不是典型的。在我的第一份（也可能是最后一份）理赔员工作中，两个人在六个月内在我的办公室心脏病发作。
 对于这里的工人来说，显而易见的解决方案似乎是以一种更有价值的方式来训练自己，但这往往比听起来听起来更难或更合理。其中一些原因可能是个人原因（如果您勉强将所有东西放在一起，很难找到从零开始获得学士学位的时间和精力）或财务上的（很高兴想到您可以学习新技能并开始新的职业） ，但是如果您勉强维持家庭的现状，那么您实际上可能无法将减薪降到入门水平。 
举个例子，2020年是我第二个月开始从事可持续工作的工作，月薪约为5万美元，是我有史以来的最高薪水。当工作被Covid中断时，我是第一个去的人-我任职时间短，公司绝对没有罕见的专业要求，因此我的职责至少可以从理论上在保留的员工之间分配。然后，我从事了抵押行业中那些高压力，高强度的工作，赚了很多钱（对我来说，一年要赚6万美元左右），但最终却出现了轻微的神经衰弱。该公司通知我，如果没有一个月的通知，我将无法使用带薪休假来处理这个休假，而且他们只是在我走了之后就让我的工作量堆积了很多，这使我处于更糟糕的境地。我设法转到了另一家公司，但是当我开始工作时，发现面试过程中的工作职责，工资和工作时间都被误传了-如果我不喜欢的话，我可以自由离开，因为这份工作将会容易填充。
 同时，我在履历表中增加了一些技能，这些技能与更好的工作和生活方式相关，我希望情况会变得更好。但是，“只是没有相关学位的技能”意味着这是一个口号-过去一个月我申请了约100个工作，但没有得到任何回应。在我可以为家人提供支持的薪水方面，只有更合格的候选人，而我可能会更擅长的极端初级职位将使我们在几个月内无法支付账单。
 对于任何人来说，这都不是件坏事，您也不必为此感到难过-我在人生中做出了很多选择，导致了这一点，而且我对自己的位置负有很多责任。但是请理解，即使您愿意牺牲劳动和时间来实现一个不确定的目标，也仍然会遇到很多运气。有些人之所以达到或低于我，是因为他们根本没有能力做得更好-并不是每个人都可以或应该学习编码，或者不能成功重返学校。
 这里的总结是，低薪工作通常由于薪资本身之外的原因而特别糟糕。工作他们的人并不愿意改变这种状况，但通常他们却无能为力，这可能是因为改变环境的手段超出了他们的控制范围，或者因为他们尝试失败。有些人懒惰且没有动力，但并非全部。
    当有人告诉我他们曾经或曾经贫穷过，而我正试图确定他们到底有多贫穷时，我问了一个常绿的问题，这个问题从来没有使我对正在处理的情况有个好主意。 。这个问题是：“他们关闭了您的水多少次？”。
 这个问题起作用的原因是因为它让我知道我正在与之交谈的人是否“感觉”坏了-即处于某种情况下，他们认为资源由于某种或其他原因而被占用-或“已经破产”，实际上实际上无法为他们维持最低限度的体面水平所需要的基本服务付费。
 一个感到破产的人可能没有很多钱，或者钱包里根本没有流动性美元。但是，仅仅没钱了并不能使您破产，因为他们会关掉您的水-这需要时间。通常，自来水公司实际要派人去关闭水源之前，您可能要迟一个月以上。即使有人感到破产，也无法立即付账（顺便说一下，这对于感到破产的人来说是很少见的；他们通常可以在某个地方拿到几百美元，在信用卡上有一些空间，等等）。这仅意味着他们需要在下个月稍微减少支出并赶上进度；在看完30天的预算后，他们可能不是麦达斯国王，但他们不会在某个早晨打开水龙头，却什么也没发现。 
显然，被打破是相反的。 您合法地无力支付账单。 同样，也不必密切关注您的预算-您已经从所有可能的地方削减了脂肪，但是您仍然没有足够的钱来使所有工作正常进行。 有时这是因为您失业了，收入枯竭了（在Covid的日子里比较普遍），有时是因为出人意料的账单导致您的预算以无法恢复的方式落后了。 但是相对于一个只不过是超支的人，破产意味着您的收入和财务存在一些结构性问题，您无法解决-所花费的只是某种程度的颠簸，使您的苹果购物车不安，现在您已经 从“挂断”转移到“问邻居”  ......
note_en:
Meta-Note: I’m sorry it’s been so long between articles; for better or worse writing here is something that I do for fun, and sometimes work and family get in the way. In this case, I was spending a lot of time trying to learn SQL; you will be pleased to note I am now able to pad my resume with “Intermediate Database Language Skills” with the best of them.
 A few years back, my wife was at a baby shower hosted by a friend by a mutual acquaintance. In a conversation with the hostess, my wife learned they were in a tough financial position - they were always broke, and no amount of budgeting seemed to help them get ahead; they had cut every cost they could and things were just getting worse and worse. She admitted to my wife that she just felt like she was sinking further and further underwater, and didn’t see any way out for her or her family.
 Note: The hostess and her husband were both doctors. They had a combined income somewhere upwards of $200,000 a year, and as the conversation developed my wife learned that their problems started and stopped with the hostess not being able to save quite as much as she’d like once the payments on their very nice house and current-year cars were made. At the time she leaned on my wife for emotional support over finances, our family of four’s income was less than $30,000 a year.
 You should know the hostess wasn’t mean-spirited in the least, and we liked her then and continue to do so. But she did have a kind of tunnel vision I’ve since noticed is increasingly common: If you came from a family that did pretty well financially, went to college and then immediately started to do pretty well yourself, it’s hard to get any kind of context for what life is like at lower income levels. This isn’t a matter of the relatively-wealthy being dumb or insensitive; it’s just legitimately difficult to get a handle on what it’s like in a life you’ve never lived, and often being legitimately confused as to why anyone would opt to make less money instead of improving their lot with training and education.
 In that spirit, I’d like to offer my services as a sort of has-been-poor guide, to fill you in on what it’s like on the other side of the tracks. In this role, I’m qualified in two ways. The first is common - we’ve never done exceptionally well financially. Things have been better in recent years as I’ve finally clawed towards the upper-end of lower-class, but Covid has reminded us how short-lived that kind of qualified success can be. We’ve had to economize in dozens of interesting ways, make hard choices here and there and sometimes/often do entirely without. It hasn’t been easy.
 At the same time, I’m mostly  happy. I have a wonderful wife who is very satisfying to be near, two kids who are about as custom-fit to my personality as possible, and dozens of friends online and off who would take a bullet for me, and vice versa. This is important because I want to you to know I’m not trying to make you feel bad - I know and understand that everyone has a different set of problems, and that I’m not unique in living in an imperfect world. I’m not trying to exaggerate problems for political points or to try to get legislation passed. Take no guilt from this article - It’s informational, not a call to arms.
    One of the bigger disconnects I run into in talking to people at or above living wage levels of income is that it’s usually assumed that the quality of things has a pretty linear association to the price. This is true at most price points, from “doing OK” all the way up to “Jeff Bezos” - if you pay a little more, you get a little more. If you pay a little less, you get a little less.
 Because this relationship is what you’d expect, you get a lot of reasonable-sounding advice that, while not exactly wrong, fails to capture the nuance of poor-people-price-mechanics very well. According to this randomly selected article,  I should really make sure not to spend more than 30% of my income on rent:
  While everyone’s circumstances are unique, many experts say it’s best to spend no more than 30% of your monthly gross income on housing-related expenses, including rent and utilities. Under that rule, it’s best to make sure that the amount you spend on rent is well below 30% of your household income. In other words, if you’re making $3,000 a month, it’s a good idea to pay no more than $900 for rent and other housing costs.
  The general thinking here is that if I spend more than 30%, I’m making myself vulnerable in all other aspects of my budget; why burden yourself with more than you can handle, when I could take a slightly worse place and be that much closer to financial health? From the perspective of the 50k a year and up crowd, this makes sense - $1250, after all, will rent a reasonable house or apartment in a lot of markets. In metropolitan Phoenix, this might look something like this:
    And this is perfectly livable - it’s very small (think >900 sqft), but otherwise it’s a well-kept place. It’s not exactly Versailles, but you wouldn’t necessarily feel like a monster telling someone to live within their means by renting an apartment here. But what happens when you are dealing with someone making $17.50 an hour? What does 840 a month get you?
    I’m not 100% sure this is a converted Motel 6, but I’m not 100% sure it’s not. And while the first apartment I showed you was in a relatively nice, livable part of town this one is in a more gun-fire prone area ominously referred to by locals as “the triangle”. Phoenix’s mostly gravel-and-concrete landscaping style cleans up nice (note the still-wet recently hosed concrete), but make no mistake: this isn’t the kind of apartment complex where hypodermic needles are exclusively owned by diabetics.
 That’s the drop-off you experience at the lower price levels - there’s nothing between “This is a tiny but acceptable apartment” and “Slum apartments in stab-ville”.
 None of this is to discount the budget advice above completely - this is absolutely the kind of apartment I should be living in right now, if I was making perfect budgetary decisions. But consider that this is a choice I have to make - do I spend 50% more on an apartment and stress my budget to death, or make my wife and kids in a place where I have 5 front doors within 15 feet of ours in the murder-iest part of town? Neither choice is great, but the lack of intermediate choices forces you to do one or the other.
 Whichever you choose, a person of less-than-intermediate income has to be prepared to stick with the rental long-term, should things not go well. This is because apartments at both of these levels quite accurately assume that you can’t afford a lawyer - while it’s normal to put down a month or two worth of rent as a security deposit, it’s much less normal to get it back; the apartment complex has no reason to give back thousands of dollars they can simply keep. This means every time you move, you pay something like a third to a month’s wages for the privilege. Since breaking a lease often means you lose your privilege to live anywhere non-hellish, this means if you don’t have cash reserves (more on these later*) at the exact right time of year, you might end up in the same place for another full year whether you like it or not.
 Note: It’s been pointed out to me that I completely forgot to talk about cash reserves and how they effect this kind of thing. It’s also been pointed out that these numbers only “make sense” in particular markets - i.e. it’s cheaper or more expensive to live other places, so the exact figures here don’t translate well for everyone. I’ll write a follow up post incorporating some of this kind of feedback soon to make up for it.
    I am always consistently shocked by how little people living at a decent-to-great income level fear their cars; it seems like they hardly lie awake at night thinking about their iffy alternator much, if at all. Scott Alexander does a pretty good job of explaining the obverse experience  here:
  When I first started working with poor patients, I was shocked how many of the problems in their lives were car-related. For well-off people like me, having a car is background noise; you buy or lease it for a reasonable price, then never worry about it again. Poor people can’t afford to buy and don’t always have good enough credit to lease. They tend to get older, sketchier cars that constantly break down. A constant complaint I heard: “My car broke, I can’t afford repairs, and I’m going to get fired if I can’t make it to my job”. Some of them can’t afford insurance and take their chances without it. Others have had various incidents with the police that cost them their license, but they can’t just not show up to work, so they drive anyway and hope they don’t get arrested.
  This is actually pretty close to the experience. It’s telling that Scott thinks the problem with a lease comes only from credit issues - a pretty bottom-barrel ford leases for 300-400 a month. For a person who makes 30,000-40000k a year, that’s something like 10-15% of their income, before we talk about insurance; couple that with the fact that you can’t squeak by on liability-only insurance in most leases, and we are already into a prohibitively expensive range.
 But there’s other things - liability insurance, as mentioned above, is often your only option that makes sense - if your car costs $2000, paying an extra $50-100 per month for high-deductible doesn’t add up; you are still $1000 out of pocket to cover the deductible in the event you need to use it. But since you can’t afford it in the first place, you don’t think about that; you just start driving insanely carefully at all times and pray any accidents you get in aren’t your fault. One slip-up and you can’t either can’t get to work at all, or you spend a lot more time doing it. Remember: Uber isn’t an option here; the ~$400 a month it would cost would bankrupt you.
 You are also more or less forced to learn to do mechanic work. I’m an administrator by trade - I usually work in the kind of jobs that have “assistant” appended to them. One of my greatest prides is typing speed, and when people ask me my hobby I tell them “Excel”. If you were judging me by my interests and natural skillset, you wouldn’t expect me to be able to change my own tires. But over the years necessity has forced me to get pretty decent at car work - I’ve done clutch rebuilds and head gasket jobs and a bunch of other miserable (for me) work that I would have rather avoided. But this was often the only option I had - it was either figuring out how to replace an alternator or do a full brake job over the weekend or lose my ability to get to the grocery store in under an hour. Keep in mind the money saved here is just the labor - parts still have a cost that’s unavoidable.
 The practical upshot of this is whenever I drive, it’s very slowly, very carefully, and listening in terror to any small noise the car makes. It’s a constant stress, and it’s limiting; we don’t go on long road trips often, and when we do my joke with my wife is usually something like “Well, if the car breaks down somewhere else, we’ll just start living there”.
 It’s reasonable to ask “If cars are so terrible, why not use public transport?”. I live in a city, and this is in fact an option that would solve many of my problems: I wouldn’t have to worry about repairs, my gasoline and insurance bills would disappear and anything that happened to the bus is the city’s problem, not mine. Especially in an emergency public transit would be a godsend and might be the difference between keeping and losing my job.
 But there’s other things to consider on this, as well. My commute isn’t short, and isn’t bus-friendly; it would go from about 30 minutes each way to just under 4 hours roundtrip. And when your commute starts and ends in a bad area, which is typical of a low-income family in a low-paying job, this means those four hours a day are spent surrounded by people who range from normal commuters at best to visibly mentally imbalanced and trying to speak to you the whole ride. I understand from talking to friends in “our mass transit is great!” towns that this isn’t the case everywhere but this is something that on average gets worse the poorer you get. I could make the commute shorter by taking a worse job (Maybe - the job market isn’t great right now), but that comes with career-limiting downsides of it’s own.
    When I’m trying to explain to my sons how a company decides what to pay someone, it usually goes something like this: A company is looking to pay a person as little as they can and keep them, so a person’s pay is determined by how rare their skills are and how much demand there is for those skills. The value of their work doesn’t factor in as much - An administrative assistant might touch every department in the company every day and facilitate a massive amount of work, but they still don’t get paid much - it’s hard to justify when you could hire and train up someone to do the same thing nearly as well with very little difficulty.
 I think this is a fairly accurate way to look at pay, but it applies to other aspects of the job. If you got sick more often than usual this year and used up your legally mandated sick days, would your company fire you for getting sick again, or demand you work sick? If the company decides it needs to cut wages, is your position the cheap-to-hire-for job the company didn’t spend a lot of money and time filling? If you don’t have rare skills, management is aware that they can ask for you to work extra hours, avoid letting you use paid time off and change your list of job duties on a whim - both they and you know that if you won’t do it, they can find somebody who will tomorrow. Not every company is bad in these ways - good people do exist - but every company is aware they could, and that tends to color every aspect of the job whether they intend it to or not.
 The worst treatment you tend to get is, perhaps counterintuitively, in jobs that approach living wage despite your low value and replaceability as a worker. These jobs tend towards needing someone who can put in a lot of hours for customer interactions that require a single point of contact; they reward intense focus over long periods of time in unpleasant situations. Another way to say this is that these are high-stress jobs with a lot of mandatory overtime and very fast pacing - they pay a lot because they can’t get people to do them otherwise. Turnover in these jobs is absurd despite the pay - some people can keep up with them but it isn’t typical. In my first (and hopefully last) claims adjuster job, two people had heart attacks in my office in a six month period.
 The obvious solution for the worker here would seem to be to train themselves in such a way as to be more valuable, but this is often a bit harder or less plausible than it sounds. Some of this can be personal reasons (if you are barely holding things together, it’s hard to find the time and energy to get a bachelors degree from scratch) or financial (It’s nice to think you could learn a new skill and start a new career, but if you are barely keeping your family fed as it is, you might not actually be able to take the pay cut dropping back to entry level would require.
 To give you an example of how this looks, 2020 started with me in my second month at a sustainable job with a salary around 50k, the most I had ever made. When the job was disrupted by Covid, I was the first to go - I had little tenure and no rare specialty absolutely required by the company, so my responsibilities could at least theoretically be divided among the retained employees. I then took one of those high-stress high-intensity jobs in the mortgage industry, made kind of a lot of money (for me, on pace to do about 60k in a year) but eventually had a minor nervous breakdown; the company informed me I couldn’t use my paid time off without a month’s notice to handle it, and that they’d simply let my workload pile up while I was gone, leaving me in a worse place. I managed to switch to another company, but found out once I started that the job duties, pay, and hours were all misrepresented in the interview process - I’m free to leave if I don’t like it, since the job will be easy to fill.
 In the meantime, I’ve added a few skills to my resume associated with better jobs and lifestyles, and I’m hopeful things get better. But “just the skill without an associated degree” means this is a slog - I’ve applied for about 100 jobs in the past month, but haven’t had a single response; there’s simply better qualified candidates at the kind of pay I can support my family with, and the kind of extremely junior positions I might have a better shot at would leave us unable to pay bills within a few months.
 This isn’t evil on anyone’s part, and you shouldn’t feel bad about it - I’ve made a lot of choices in my life that led to this point and I have a lot of responsibility in terms of where I find myself. But understand that even if you are willing to sacrifice the labor and time it takes to work towards an uncertain goal, there’s still a fair amount of luck involved. And some people are at my level or below it because they simply lack the capability to do better - not everyone can or should learn to code, or would be able to successfully go back to school.
 The summary here is that low-paying jobs are often uniquely bad for reasons beyond the pay itself. The people who work them aren’t unwilling to change this, but often they are unable to, either because the means to change their circumstance is outside of their control or because their attempts to do so fail. Some are lazy and unmotivated, but not all.
    When someone is telling me they are or have been poor and I’m trying to determine  how poor exactly they were, there’s one evergreen question I ask that has never failed to give me a good idea of what kind of situation I’m dealing with. That question is: “How many times have they turned off your water?”.
 The reason this question works is because it lets me know whether the person I’m talking to has “felt” broke - I.E. been in a situation where they felt resource strapped for some reason or another - or has “been broke”, actually factually unable to pay for basic services they needed to survive at a minimally decent human level.
 A person who feels broke might not have a lot of money, or might not have any liquid dollars in their wallet at all. But just being out of money doesn’t make you broke in the sense that they turn off your water - that takes time. Usually you can be over a month late before the water company actually goes to the trouble of sending someone out to turn off your water. Even if someone feels broke and can’t immediately pay their bills (this is rare for people who feel broke, by the way; they usually have a few hundred dollars they can get at somewhere, some space on a credit card, etc.) this only means they need to cut their spending somewhat the next month and catch up; they might not be King Midas after watching their budget for 30 days, but they won’t turn the tap some morning and finds nothing comes out.
 Obviously being broke is the opposite; you are legitimately out of funds you need to cover a bill. It’s not a matter of watching your budget closer, either - you’ve cut the fat from every possible place and you still just don’t have enough to make everything work. Sometimes this is because you lost a job and your income dried up (more common these Covid days) and sometimes it’s because a surprise bill caused your budget to get behind in a way you couldn’t recover from. But as opposed to someone who simply overspent, being broke implies you have some structural problem with your income and finances that you can’t fix - all it took was a small bump of some kind to upset your apple cart, and now you’ve moved from “hanging on” to “asking your neighb

跳转到导航跳转搜索Alcubierre驱动器，Alcubierre翘曲驱动器或Alcubierre度量标准（指公制张量）是一种基于墨西哥爱因斯坦的物理学家提出的广义相对论场方程解的推测性翘曲驱动器思想。 Miguel Alcubierre在加的夫威尔士大学攻读博士学位期间，如果可以创建一个可配置的能量密度场低于真空场（即负质量），则航天器可以实现明显快于光的行进。 。 [1] [2]
 航天器将通过收缩其前方的空间并扩大其后方的空间来遍历距离，而不是超过局部参考框架内的光速，从而实现比光速更快的旅行。在正常时空内，物体无法加速到光速；取而代之的是，Alcubierre驱动器可移动物体周围的空间，从而使物体比正常空间中的光线更快地到达目的地，而不会违反任何物理定律。 [3]
 尽管Alcubierre提出的度量标准与爱因斯坦场方程是一致的，但这种驱动器的构造并不一定是可能的。拟议的Alcubierre驱动器机制意味着能量密度为负，因此需要奇特的物质。如果不存在具有正确属性的异物，则无法构造驱动器。然而，在其原始文章的结尾处，[4]，Alcubierre认为（遵循物理学家分析可穿越的虫洞的论点[5] [6]），平行板之间的Casimir真空可以满足Alcubierre驱动器的负能量要求。
 另一个可能的问题是，尽管Alcubierre度量与爱因斯坦方程一致，但广义相对论并未纳入量子力学。一些物理学家提出了一些论点，表明量子引力理论（将同时包含这两种理论）将消除那些广义相对论中允许向后移动的解决方案（请参见时间保护猜想），从而使Alcubierre驱动无效。
   1994年，Miguel Alcubierre提出了一种通过产生波浪来改变空间几何形状的方法，该波浪将导致航天器前方的空间结构收缩，其后方的空间膨胀。 [4] [1] [2]然后，该船将在波浪形的平坦区域内乘坐此波，并且不会在该气泡内移动，而是随着该区域自身的移动而随行而行的驱动器。直到Harold Sonny White [7] [8]指出，如果翘曲气泡的壁较厚，可以减少所需的能量，这被认为会使用过多的负能量。其他人做了其他改进，总结如下。
  Alcubierre度量标准定义了扭曲驱动器的时空。它是一个洛伦兹流形，如果在广义相对论的背景下进行解释，它会允许扭曲气泡出现在以前平坦的时空中，并以比光速更快的速度移开。气泡的内部是一个惯性参考系，居民没有适当的加速度。这种运输方法不涉及相对于变形气泡的内容以高于光速的速度运动的对象；也就是说，扭曲气泡内的光束始终会比飞船移动得更快。由于气泡内的物体运动的速度不是局部地快于光，因此Alcubierre度量的数学公式与相对论的传统主张是一致的（即，具有质量的物体不能达到或超过光速） ）和常规相对论效应（例如时间膨胀）将不会像在近光速下的常规运动那样适用。
 但是，Alcubierre驱动器仍然是一个假想概念，似乎存在难题，尽管不再认为所需的能量过大。 [9]

  因此，由于能量密度为负，因此人们需要异物才能比光速更快地传播。 [4]从理论上讲，并不排除外来物质的存在；但是，产生和维持足够多的异物来执行壮举，例如光速旅行（并保持虫洞的咽喉张开）是不切实际的。据作家罗伯特·劳特（Robert Low）称，在广义相对论的背景下，如果没有外来物质，就不可能构建扭曲的驱动力。 [10]
   来自牛津大学的天体物理学家杰米·法恩斯（Jamie Farnes）提出了一种理论，该理论发表在同行评议的科学杂志《天文学与物理学》上。天体物理学将暗能量和暗物质统一为单一的暗流体，并且可以在2030年左右通过新的科学仪器进行测试。[11]法恩斯发现爱因斯坦在开发引力斥力负质量的方程时探索了引力排斥性负质量的概念。广义相对论，一个导致“美丽”的想法宇宙具有相等数量的正负品质的假设。法恩斯该理论依赖于负质量，该负质量的行为与Alcubierre驱动器的物理学相同，从而为当前的“宇宙学危机”提供了自然的解决方案。由于时变的哈勃参数。 [12]
 作为法恩斯从理论上讲，正质量（即船）达到的速度等于光速，因此被称为“争议性”。 [13]如果该理论是正确的，并且已在科学文献中引起了激烈的争论，它将解释暗能量，暗物质，允许闭合的类似时间的曲线（请参阅时间旅行），并建议对异物进行Alcubierre驱动在物理上是可能的。 [14]
  关于狭义相对论的某些特定影响，例如洛伦兹收缩和时间膨胀，Alcubierre度量显然具有一些特殊的方面。尤其是，Alcubierre已显示使用Alcubierre驱动器的船舶即使在扭曲气泡加速的情况下也能以自由落体测地线行驶：其船员在加速的同时会自由下落，而不会经历加速g力。然而，由于那里的大空间曲率，巨大的潮汐力将出现在平坦空间容积的边缘附近，但是适当的规格规格将使潮汐力在船所占据的容积内保持很小。 [4] 
原始的扭曲驱动度量及其简单变体恰好具有ADM形式，该形式通常用于讨论广义相对论的初值公式。这可能解释了普遍的误解，认为这种时空是广义相对论场方程的解。 ADM形式的度量标准适用于某些惯性观测器系列，但是这些观测器在物理上并没有真正区别于其他此类观测器。阿尔库比尔（Alcubierre）解释了他的“扭曲泡沫”。泡沫之前的空间收缩和泡沫之后的扩张，但这种解释可能会产生误导性，[15]因为收缩和扩张实际上是指附近ADM观察员家庭成员的相对运动。 [需要引用]
 在广义相对论中，通常首先指定物质和能量的合理分布，然后找到与之相关的时空的几何形状。但是也可以在另一个方向上运行爱因斯坦场方程，首先指定一个度量，然后找到与其关联的能量-动量张量，这就是Alcubierre在建立其度量时所做的。这种做法意味着解决方案可能违反各种能源条件，并需要异物。对奇异物质的需求引发了一个疑问，即人们是否可以在缺少扭曲气泡的初始时空中分配该物质，从而在稍后的时间创建气泡，尽管一些物理学家已经提出了动态扭曲驱动时空的模型。在先前平坦的空间中会形成翘曲气泡。 [16]而且，根据Serguei Krasnikov的观点，[17]在以前平坦的空间中为单向FTL旅行产生气泡需要迫使异物以局部快于光速的速度运动，这需要存在尽管克拉斯尼科夫还指出，从一开始时空时就不平坦的情况下，通过在行进路径上预先放置一些设备并对它们进行编程以使其在预定的时刻开始运行并在预先指定的方式。一些建议的方法避免了速动运动的问题，但可能会在气泡的前面产生裸露的奇点。 [18] [19]艾伦·埃弗里特（Allen Everett）和托马斯·罗曼（Thomas Roman）对克拉斯尼科夫的发现发表评论（克拉斯尼科夫管）：
 [发现]并不意味着Alcubierre气泡（如果有可能产生的话）不能用作超光速旅行的手段。这仅意味着更改度量标准并创建气泡所需的操作必须由某些观察者事先采取，该观察者的前向光锥包含气泡的整个轨迹。 [20]
 例如，如果要根据外部时钟前往Deneb（相距2600光年）并在未来不到2600年到达，则需要有人至少已经开始着手将地球从太空过渡到Deneb 2600年前：
 然后，相对于气泡轨迹适当定位的太空飞船可以选择进入气泡，就像乘客搭上一辆经过的手推车一样，从而进行超光速旅程....正如克拉斯尼科夫指出的那样，因果关系因素并不能阻止机组人员宇宙飞船通过自己的动作来安排从地球到遥远恒星的往返行程，并在任意短的时间内完成飞行（按地球上的时钟测量），方法是改变沿其出站行程的距离。 [20]
  在共形引力的框架内，广义相对论的扩展是局部保留了时空的角度，Alcubierre度量标准并未违反特定时空形状的弱能量条件，因此，光速旅行不需要外来物质。 [21]
  这种形式的度量有很大的困难，因为所有已知的翘曲驱动时空理论都违反了各种能量条件。 [22]然而，通过利用某些经实验验证的量子现象（例如卡西米尔效应），可以实现Alcubierre型翘曲驱动，当这种现象产生时，应力-能量张量也违反了能量条件，例如负质量-能量。在量子场论的背景下进行了描述。 [23] [24]

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  The  Alcubierre drive,  Alcubierre warp drive, or  Alcubierre metric (referring to  metric tensor) is a speculative  warp drive idea based on a solution of  Einstein's field equations in  general relativity as proposed by Mexican theoretical physicist  Miguel Alcubierre during his PhD study at the University of Wales, Cardiff, by which a spacecraft could achieve apparent  faster-than-light travel if a configurable  energy-density field lower than that of the  vacuum (that is,  negative mass) could be created.  [1]  [2]
 Rather than exceeding the  speed of light within a  local reference frame, a spacecraft would traverse distances by contracting space in front of it and expanding space behind it, resulting in effective faster-than-light travel. Objects cannot accelerate to the speed of light within normal  spacetime; instead, the Alcubierre drive shifts space around an object so that the object would arrive at its destination more quickly than light would in normal space without breaking any  physical laws.  [3]
 Although the metric proposed by Alcubierre is consistent with the Einstein field equations, construction of such a drive is not necessarily possible. The proposed mechanism of the Alcubierre drive implies a negative  energy density and therefore requires  exotic matter. If exotic matter with the correct properties cannot exist, then the drive cannot be constructed. At the close of his original article,  [4] however, Alcubierre argued (following an argument developed by physicists analyzing traversable  wormholes  [5]  [6]) that the  Casimir vacuum between parallel plates could fulfill the negative-energy requirement for the Alcubierre drive.
 Another possible issue is that, although the Alcubierre metric is consistent with Einstein's equations, general relativity does not incorporate  quantum mechanics. Some physicists have presented arguments to suggest that a theory of  quantum gravity (which would incorporate both theories) would eliminate those solutions in general relativity that allow for backwards time travel ( see the  chronology protection conjecture) and thus make the Alcubierre drive invalid.
   In 1994,  Miguel Alcubierre proposed a method for changing the  geometry of space by creating a wave that would cause the fabric of space ahead of a  spacecraft to contract and the space behind it to expand.  [4]  [1]  [2] The ship would then ride this wave inside a region of flat space, known as a  warp bubble, and would not move within this bubble but instead be carried along as the region itself moves due to the actions of the drive. It was thought to use too much negative energy until  Harold Sonny White  [7]  [8] showed that the amount of energy required could be reduced if the warp bubble's wall was thicker. Others have made other refinements which are summarised below.
  The Alcubierre  metric defines the warp-drive  spacetime. It is a  Lorentzian manifold that, if interpreted in the context of  general relativity, allows a warp bubble to appear in previously flat spacetime and move away at effectively faster-than-light speed. The interior of the bubble is an  inertial reference frame and inhabitants experience no proper acceleration. This method of transport does not involve objects in motion at faster-than-light speeds with respect to the contents of the warp bubble; that is, a light beam within the warp bubble would still always move more quickly than the ship. Because objects within the bubble are not moving (locally) more quickly than light, the mathematical formulation of the Alcubierre metric is consistent with the conventional claims of the laws of relativity (namely, that an object with mass cannot attain or exceed the speed of light) and conventional  relativistic effects such as  time dilation would not apply as they would with conventional motion at near-light speeds.
 The Alcubierre drive, however, remains a hypothetical concept with seemingly difficult problems, though the amount of energy required is no longer thought to be unobtainably large.  [9]
  Using the  ADM formalism of  general relativity, the  spacetime is described by a  foliation of space-like  hypersurfaces of constant coordinate time  t, with the metric taking the following general form:
 d    s    2    =  −    (      α    2    −    β    i      β    i     )     d    t    2    +  2    β    i      d    x    i      d  t  +    γ    i  j      d    x    i      d    x    j    ,    {\displaystyle ds^{2}=-\left(\alpha ^{2}-\beta _{i}\beta ^{i}\right)\,dt^{2}+2\beta _{i}\,dx^{i}\,dt+\gamma _{ij}\,dx^{i}\,dx^{j},}
  α is the lapse function that gives the interval of proper time between nearby hypersurfaces,
 β i is the shift vector that relates the spatial coordinate systems on different hypersurfaces,
  α       =  1  ,          β    x         =  −    v    s    (  t  )  f      (      r    s    (  t  )      )    ,          β    y         =    β    z    =  0  ,          γ    i  j         =    δ    i  j    ,        {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\alpha &=1,\\\beta ^{x}&=-v_{s}(t)f{\big (}r_{s}(t){\big )},\\\beta ^{y}&=\beta ^{z}=0,\\\gamma _{ij}&=\delta _{ij},\end{aligned}}}
  v    s    (  t  )       =        d    x    s    (  t  )     d  t     ,          r    s    (  t  )       =          (    x  −    x    s    (  t  )        )      2    +    y    2    +    z    2      ,        f  (    r    s    )       =        tanh  ⁡      (    σ  (    r    s    +  R  )      )    −  tanh  ⁡      (    σ  (    r    s    −  R  )      )       2  tanh  ⁡  (  σ  R  )     ,        {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}v_{s}(t)&={\frac {dx_{s}(t)}{dt}},\\r_{s}(t)&={\sqrt {{\big (}x-x_{s}(t){\big )}^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}},\\f(r_{s})&={\frac {\tanh {\big (}\sigma (r_{s}+R){\big )}-\tanh {\big (}\sigma (r_{s}-R){\big )}}{2\tanh(\sigma R)}},\end{aligned}}}
 with arbitrary parameters   R > 0 and   σ > 0. Alcubierre's specific form of the metric can thus be written
 d    s    2    =    (      v    s    (  t    )    2    f      (      r    s    (  t  )        )      2    −  1   )     d    t    2    −  2    v    s    (  t  )  f      (      r    s    (  t  )      )      d  x    d  t  +  d    x    2    +  d    y    2    +  d    z    2    .    {\displaystyle ds^{2}=\left(v_{s}(t)^{2}f{\big (}r_{s}(t){\big )}^{2}-1\right)\,dt^{2}-2v_{s}(t)f{\big (}r_{s}(t){\big )}\,dx\,dt+dx^{2}+dy^{2}+dz^{2}.}
 With this particular form of the metric, it can be shown that the energy density measured by observers whose 4-velocity is normal to the hypersurfaces is given by
 −        c    4      8  π  G             v    s     2      (      y    2    +    z    2     )      4    g    2      r    s     2           (        d  f     d    r    s       )     2    ,    {\displaystyle -{\frac {c^{4}}{8\pi G}}{\frac {v_{s}^{2}\left(y^{2}+z^{2}\right)}{4g^{2}r_{s}^{2}}}\left({\frac {df}{dr_{s}}}\right)^{2},}
  Thus, because the energy density is negative, one needs  exotic matter to travel more quickly than the speed of light.  [4] The existence of exotic matter is not theoretically ruled out; however, generating and sustaining enough exotic matter to perform feats such as faster-than-light travel (and to keep open the "throat" of a  wormhole) is thought to be impractical. [   citation needed] According to writer Robert Low, within the context of general relativity it is impossible to construct a  warp drive in the absence of exotic matter.  [10]
   The astrophysicist  Jamie Farnes from the  University of Oxford has proposed a theory, published in the peer-reviewed scientific journal  Astronomy & Astrophysics, that unifies  dark energy and  dark matter into a single  dark fluid, and which can be tested by new scientific instruments in circa 2030.  [11] Farnes found that  Albert Einstein had explored the idea of gravitationally repulsive negative masses while developing the equations of  general relativity, an idea which leads to a "beautiful" hypothesis where the  cosmos has equal amounts of positive and negative qualities. Farnes' theory relies on  negative masses that behave identically to the physics of the Alcubierre drive, providing a natural solution for the current "crisis in cosmology" due to a time-variable  Hubble parameter.  [12]
 As Farnes' theory allows a positive mass (i.e. a ship) to reach a speed equal to the speed of light, it has been dubbed "controversial".  [13] If the theory is correct, which has been highly debated in the scientific literature, it would explain dark energy, dark matter, allow  closed timelike curves (see  time travel), and suggest that an Alcubierre drive is physically possible with exotic matter.  [14]
  With regard to certain specific effects of special relativity, such as  Lorentz contraction and  time dilation, the Alcubierre metric has some apparently peculiar aspects. In particular, Alcubierre has shown that a ship using an Alcubierre drive travels on a free-fall geodesic even while the warp bubble is accelerating: its crew would be in free fall while accelerating without experiencing accelerational  g-forces. Enormous  tidal forces, however, would be present near the edges of the flat-space volume because of the large space curvature there, but suitable specification of the metric would keep the tidal forces very small within the volume occupied by the ship.  [4]
 The original warp-drive metric and simple variants of it happen to have the  ADM form, which is often used in discussing the initial-value formulation of general relativity. This might explain the widespread misconception that this spacetime is a  solution of the field equation of general relativity. [   citation needed] Metrics in ADM form are  adapted to a certain family of inertial observers, but these observers are not really physically distinguished from other such families. Alcubierre interpreted his "warp bubble" in terms of a contraction of space ahead of the bubble and an expansion behind, but this interpretation could be misleading,  [15] since the contraction and expansion actually refer to the relative motion of nearby members of the family of ADM observers. [   citation needed]
 In general relativity, one often first specifies a plausible distribution of matter and energy, and then finds the geometry of the spacetime associated with it; but it is also possible to run the  Einstein field equations in the other direction, first specifying a metric and then finding the  energy–momentum tensor associated with it, and this is what Alcubierre did in building his metric. This practice means that the solution can violate various  energy conditions and require  exotic matter. The need for exotic matter raises questions about whether one can distribute the matter in an initial spacetime that lacks a warp bubble in such a way that the bubble is created at a later time, although some physicists have proposed models of dynamical warp-drive spacetimes in which a warp bubble is formed in a previously flat space.  [16] Moreover, according to  Serguei Krasnikov,  [17] generating a bubble in a previously flat space for a  one-way FTL trip requires forcing the exotic matter to move at local faster-than-light speeds, something that would require the existence of  tachyons, although Krasnikov also notes that when the spacetime is not flat from the outset, a similar result could be achieved without tachyons by placing in advance some devices along the travel path and programming them to come into operation at preassigned moments and to operate in a preassigned manner. Some suggested methods avoid the problem of tachyonic motion, but would probably generate a  naked singularity at the front of the bubble.  [18]  [19] Allen Everett and Thomas Roman comment on Krasnikov's finding ( Krasnikov tube):
 [The finding] does not mean that Alcubierre bubbles, if it were possible to create them, could not be used as a means of superluminal travel. It only means that the actions required to change the metric and create the bubble must be taken beforehand by some observer whose forward  light cone contains the entire trajectory of the bubble.  [20]
 For example, if one wanted to travel to  Deneb (2,600 light years away) and arrive less than 2,600 years in the future according to external clocks, it would be required that someone had already begun work on warping the space from Earth to Deneb at least 2,600 years ago:
 A spaceship appropriately located with respect to the bubble trajectory could then choose to enter the bubble, rather like a passenger catching a passing trolley car, and thus make the superluminal journey ... as Krasnikov points out, causality considerations do not prevent the crew of a spaceship from arranging, by their own actions, to complete a  round trip from Earth to a distant star and back in an arbitrarily short time, as measured by clocks on Earth, by altering the metric along the path of their outbound trip.  [20]
  Within the framework of  conformal gravity, an extension of  general relativity in which the angles of spacetime are locally preserved, the Alcubierre metric does not violate the weak energy condition for particular spacetime shapes, and hence faster-than-light travel would not require  exotic matter.  [21]
  The metric of this form has significant difficulties because all known warp-drive spacetime theories violate various  energy conditions.  [22] Nevertheless, an Alcubierre-type warp drive might be realized by exploiting certain experimentally verified quantum phenomena, such as the  Casimir effect, that lead to  stress–energy tensors that also violate the energy conditions, such as negative  mass–energy, when described in the context of the quantum field theories.  [23]  [24]
  If certain  quantum inequalities conjectured by Ford and Roman hold,  [25] the energy requirements for some warp drives may be unfeasibly large as well as negative. For example, the energy equivalent of −10 64 kg might be required  [26] to transport a small spaceship across the Milky Way—an amount orders of magnitude greater than the estimated  mass of the observable universe. Counterarguments to these apparent problems have also been offered.  [3]
 Chris Van den Broeck of the  Katholieke Universiteit Leuven in Belgium, in 1999, tried to address the potential issues.  [27] By contracting the 3+1-dimensional surface area of the bubble being transported by the drive, while at the same time expanding the three-dimensional volume contained inside, Van den Broeck was able to reduce the total energy needed to transport small atoms to less than three  solar masses. Later, by slightly modifying the Van den Broeck metric,  Serguei Krasnikov reduced the necessary total amount of  negative mass to a few milligrams.  [3]  [22] Van den Broeck detailed this by saying that the total energy can be reduced dramatically by keeping the surface area of the warp bubble itself microscopically small, while at the same time expanding the spatial volume inside the bubble. However, Van den Broeck concludes that the energy densities required are still unachievable, as are the small size (a few orders of magnitude above the  Planck scale) of the spacetime structures needed.  [18]
 In 2012, physicist  Harold White and collaborators announced that modifying the geometry of exotic matter could reduce the mass–energy requirements for a macroscopic space ship from the equivalent of the planet  Jupiter to that of the  Voyager 1 spacecraft (c. 700 kg)  [9] or less,  [28] and stated their intent to perform small-scale experiments in constructing warp fields.  [9] White proposed to thicken the extremely thin wall of the warp bubble, so the energy is focused in a larger volume, but the overall peak energy density is actually smaller. In a flat 2D representation, the ring of positive and negative energy, initially very thin, becomes a larger, fuzzy donut shape. However, as this less energetic warp bubble also thickens toward the interior region, it leaves less flat space to house the spacecraft, which has to be smaller.  [29] Furthermore, if the intensity of the space warp can be oscillated over time, the energy required is reduced even more.  [9] According to White, a modified  Michelson–Morley interferometer could test the idea: one of the legs of the interferometer would appear to have a slightly different length when the test devices were energised.  [28]
  Krasnikov proposed that if  tachyonic matter cannot be found or used, then a solution might be to arrange for masses along the path of the vessel to be set in motion in such a way that the required field was produced. But in this case, the Alcubierre drive vessel can only travel routes that, like a railroad, have first been equipped with the necessary infrastructure. The pilot inside the bubble is causally disconnected with its walls and cannot carry out any action outside the bubble: the bubble cannot be used for the first trip to a distant star because the pilot cannot place infrastructure ahead of the bubble while "in transit". For example, travelling to  Vega (which is 25 light-years from Earth) requires arranging everything so that the bubble moving toward Vega with a superluminal velocity would appear; such arrangements will always take more than 25 years.  [17]
 Coule has argued that schemes, such as the one proposed by Alcubierre, are infeasible because matter placed  en route of the intended path of a craft must be placed at superluminal speed—that constructing an Alcubierre drive requires an Alcubierre drive even if the metric that allows it is physically meaningful. Coule further argues that an analogous objection will apply to  any proposed method of constructing an Alcubierre drive.  [19]
  An article by José Natário (2002) argues that crew members could not control, steer or stop the ship in its warp bubble because the ship could not send signals to the front of the bubble (the Horizon problem).  [30] Fernando Loup in 2013  [31] argued that the Horizon problem could be overcome in the Natário warp drive, and thus a causally-connected and therefore controllable warp bubble could be established by crew members sending information forward via micro-warp bubbles of the nature argued for by Gauthier, Gravel, and Melanson,  [32]  [33] drawing on previous work by Pfenning and Ford.  [34]
 A 2009 article by Carlos Barceló, Stefano Finazzi, and Stefano Liberati uses quantum theory to argue that the Alcubierre drive at faster-than-light velocities is impossible mostly because extremely high temperatures caused by  Hawking radiation would destroy anything inside the bubble at superluminal velocities and destabilize the bubble itself; the article also argues that these problems are absent if the bubble velocity is subluminal, although the drive still requires exotic matter.  [16]
  Brendan McMonigal,  Geraint F. Lewis, and Philip O'Byrne have argued that were an Alcubierre-driven ship to decelerate from superluminal speed, the particles that its bubble had gathered in transit would be released in energetic outbursts akin to the infinitely-blueshifted radiation hypothesized to occur at the inner event horizon of a  Kerr black hole; forward-facing particles would thereby be energetic enough to destroy anything at the destination directly in front of the ship.  [35]  [36]
  The amount of negative energy required for such a propulsion is not yet known. Pfenning and Allen Everett of  Tufts hold that a warp bubble traveling at 10-times the speed of light must have a wall thickness of no more than 10 −32 meters—close to the limiting  Planck length,  1.6 × 10 −35 meters.  [37] In Alcubierre's original calculations, a bubble macroscopically large enough to enclose a ship of 200 meters would require a total amount of exotic matter greater than the mass of the observable universe, and straining the exotic matter to an extremely thin band of 10

微软首席执行官比尔·盖茨对谁应该购买比特币有一个有趣的看法。他是全球第三大富翁，他说比特币并不适合他，因为他的钱比特斯拉首席执行官埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）少。因此，基本上任何人比马斯克少钱都不应投资于比特币。马斯克对他对比特币的热情一直很强烈。他的公司特斯拉最近在加密货币上投资了15亿美元。
  盖茨在接受彭博电视台采访时曾表示，比特币并不适合所有人，只有世界上最富有的人才应该考虑投资。
  埃隆（Elon）有很多钱，他非常老练，所以我不用担心他的比特币会随机上升或下降。有足够的余钱。我的一般想法是，如果您的钱少于埃隆（Elon），您可能应该提防，＆＃34;盖茨在接受采访时说。
   在特斯拉购买15亿美元的货币并接受其作为支付方式之后，比特币的价值翻了一番。在他的公司进行一项名为比特币的投资后，马斯克很快就拥有了比现金更愚蠢的流动性。
  特斯拉的10-K报告向美国证券交易委员会（SEC）确认，特斯拉已在比特币加密货币上投资了15亿美元。 ＆＃34;根据这项政策，我们对比特币进行了总计15亿美元的投资，并且可能会不时或长期收购和持有数字资产。此外，我们希望在不久的将来，根据适用法律并在有限的基础上，开始在有限的基础上接受比特币作为我们产品的付款方式，无论收到后我们可能会清算，也可能不会清算，＆＃34;该公司在报告中说。但是，马斯克警告用户不要在单独的视频中将毕生积蓄的时间花在加密货币上。
  虽然盖茨只希望像Musk这样的富人投资比特币，但后者建议人们不要将毕生积蓄投资于加密货币。显然，人们不应该将毕生积蓄投资在加密货币上-这是不明智的，麝香说。加密货币很有可能是地球的未来货币，就像它会变成哪个？也许会是多个。此时应考虑投机。因此，不要在加密投机方面走得太远，麝香添加。

note_en:
Microsoft CEO Bill Gates has an interesting take on who should buy Bitcoins. He being the third richest man in the world said that Bitcoins are not for him because he has less money than Tesla CEO Elon Musk. So basically anybody who has less money than Musk should not invest in Bitcoin. Musk has been very vocal about his passion for Bitcoin. His company Tesla recently invested $1.5 billion in cryptocurrency.
  Gates during an interview with Bloomberg TV had said that Bitcoin is not for everyone and only the richest person in the world should consider investing in it.
  "Elon has tons of money and he's very sophisticated, so I don't worry that his Bitcoin will sort of randomly go up or down.I do think people get bought into these manias who may not have as much money to spare. My general thought would be that if you have less money than Elon, you should probably watch out," Gates said in the interview.
   Bitcoin's value doubled after Tesla purchased $1.5bn of the currency and accepted it as a mode of payment. Musk soon after his company made an investment called Bitcoin a less dumb form of liquidity than cash.
  Tesla's 10-K report confirmed with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) that Tesla has invested $1.5 billion in the Bitcoin cryptocurrency. "We invested an aggregate $1.50 billion in bitcoin under this policy and may acquire and hold digital assets from time to time or long-term. Moreover, we expect to begin accepting bitcoin as a form of payment for our products in the near future, subject to applicable laws and initially on a limited basis, which we may or may not liquidate upon receipt," the company said in the report. However, Musk had warned users to not spend all their life savings on cryptocurrency in a separate video.
  While Gates only wants rich people like Musk to invest in Bitcoin, the latter advised people to not invest their life savings in cryptocurrency. "People should not invest their life savings in cryptocurrency, to be clear -- that's unwise," Musk said. "There's a good chance that crypto is the future currency of Earth, and it's like which one's it going to be? Maybe it'll be multiple. It should be considered speculation at this point. So don't go too far with the crypto speculation front," Musk added.

拜登总统努力在4月底前将至少1亿剂COVID-19疫苗注射到美国人民的怀抱中，白宫于2月9日宣布，它将开始向1300家具有联邦资格的社区卫生中心运送疫苗。这些组织主要为贫困线以下的黑人和棕色社区患者提供服务。美国政府周二宣布了计划，将其向零售药房发送的疫苗数量增加一倍，达到200万剂。这些努力增加了对医院，州和市政机构的现有分配。但是，特朗普政府缺乏消息灵通的消息，再加上一系列不同的COVID疫苗，新的冠状病毒变种的出现以及各州和市政的推广计划不一致，导致了混乱并导致疫苗犹豫。 《科学美国人》请巴尔的摩约翰霍普金斯大学医学院的药师NamandjéBumpus和新加坡杜克大学-国大医学院的免疫学家Ashley Lauren St. John回答了有关目前可用的COVID疫苗的一些最大问题。
   是的！如果您当前有资格获得疫苗，请继续进行。我们确实需要更多的人进行疫苗接种，以增强牛群免疫力并减缓疾病的传播。疫苗是我们工具包中使我们摆脱大流行病的重要组成部分。如果您有个人犹豫的原因，请咨询您的医疗保健提供者。
  美国疾病预防控制中心没有建议从COVID康复到接种疫苗之间的最短间隔。您的症状应该消失，并且在获得疫苗之前应该从隔离区中释放出来，以免将COVID透露给其他任何人。确实可以等待更长的时间，因为您已经建立了一些免疫反应-如果您认为适合您，请与您的医疗保健提供者联系。 [编者注：与大多数公共卫生指南相反，白宫首席医学顾问安东尼·福西（Anthony Fauci）表示，他认为感染者应等待90天后再接种疫苗。]
 您应该如何解释疫苗功效-“ 95％功效”或“ 66％功效”甚至意味着什么？
 疫苗效力是指在精心控制的试验中其表现如何，而效力则描述了其在现实世界中的表现。一个常见的错误是将95％的功效解释为即使您接种了疫苗也有5％的机会生病。那根本不是真的。功效是根据未接种安慰剂对照组的试验计算得出的，在试验结束时，他们查看了以有症状COVID结束的对照组的数字，以得出基线感染率。例如，辉瑞-BioNTech疫苗在其临床试验中具有95％的功效。该数字来自以下事实：安慰剂组有162人出现了症状性COVID，而疫苗接种组中有8人出现了症状。总共有170例。八分之一大约是170％的百分之五。本质上，这就是计算功效的方式。大多数COVID疫苗临床试验都报告了预防任何症状性疾病（不一定是严重疾病）的功效。但是，后者的早期数据很有希望。
 如果您仍然接种疫苗并且仍然受到感染，疫苗是否仍会有所作为？它可以预防严重的疾病或死亡吗？
 是的，我们对大多数当前疫苗所见的症状较轻，被感染的疫苗接种者几乎没有住院和死亡。几项疫苗试验已经观察到，接种疫苗的受试者的严重疾病有所减轻。除少数例外（例如南非的阿斯利康疫苗）外，这些疫苗似乎降低了您出现有症状COVID的风险，但如果确实得到，那很可能不会那么严重。 
疫苗是否能抵抗新的病毒变种，包括在英国，南非和巴西首次发现的那些变种？
 迄今为止的数据表明，大多数疫苗的确为新变种提供了至少某种保护。从一些临床试验数据和抗体中和研究中我们可以看到，针对原始病毒株设计的疫苗对某些新变体的效果可能不那么有效，尤其是针对B.1.351变体中发现的突变。现在在南非很普遍。许多新变种的刺突蛋白都发生了变化，该病毒用于感染细胞，因此与疫苗接种后的人的身体有些不同。总体而言，美国授权的疫苗仍能达到功效目标，并且似乎可以抵御新的变种。防止出现新变异的最佳方法是让很多人接种疫苗。 Moderna正在开发助推器，以在需要时提供额外的保护，而辉瑞BioNTech也正在探索这种可能性。
   mRNA疫苗（例如由Pfizer-BioNTech或Moderna生产的疫苗）提供了遗传指导，以脂质包裹的形式将单链RNA形式的病毒蛋白生产到您的细胞中，而无需自身注射任何病毒。这具有一些令人兴奋的优势，因为它仅包含您希望免疫系统识别的那些病毒成分，并且使您自己的细胞模仿自然感染病毒的细胞中病毒蛋白的制造方式。它非常擅长诱导与自然感染相同类型的免疫反应。不利的一面是mRNA需要保持非常低的温度才能使其稳定。
 病毒载体疫苗-例如阿斯利康（AstraZeneca）或强生（Johnson＆amp; amp;）生产的那些约翰逊（Johnson）-使用双链DNA代替RNA，但仍在向您的细胞传递遗传指令。他们使用改良的安全病毒（非SARS-CoV-2）来提供制造病毒蛋白的说明。
  选择不同的疫苗设计是因为它们具有独特的优势，因此没有“最好的”疫苗，但是对于某些人群而言，可能会有更好的疫苗。 mRNA疫苗和病毒载体疫苗都可以指导您的细胞制造冠状病毒刺突蛋白碎片，以便下次您的身体遇到这些蛋白时，便可以引发免疫反应。功效数字来自在不同环境下进行的不同临床研究，因此，与其比较这些数字，不如说是经批准的疫苗通常可以起作用。他们有功效。
  我们希望如此，但我们还不完全了解。疫苗有两个主要目标：保护个人和制止疾病的传播。基于我们对病毒的了解和一些新兴数据，我们确实有理由希望，但是我们仍然不确定疫苗接种是否会减少疾病传播。因此，即使您已经接种了疫苗，继续保持社交距离，戴口罩并遵守其他指南以防止疾病传播，这一点很重要。
  这可能发生。疫苗通常需要加强剂量才能达到最佳保护水平。有些疫苗可以保护某人一生，而另一些则需要每隔几年增加一次。重要的是要随着时间的推移监视接种疫苗者的免疫反应，这样我们才能知道是否以及何时需要加强免疫。当前大多数可用的COVID疫苗都需要两次剂量才能完全有效。由于可获得性有限，疾病预防控制中心最近扩大了两次剂量之间的建议允许间隔，尽管一些专家对此决定提出了批评。 
对注射的任何疫苗或药物都可能发生过敏反应，但这种情况极为罕见。疫苗应始终在医疗保健提供者可以治疗任何意料不到的过敏反应的环境中施用。如果人们对食物过敏或对乳胶过敏，则无需担心疫苗。大多数人无需担心过敏反应。对于那些有严重过敏反应或对疫苗成分过敏的人，最好在获得COVID疫苗之前与医生讨论。
  常见的副作用是我们对其他疫苗所期望的副作用：疲劳，头痛，发烧（尤其是在第二剂注射后）以及注射部位的急性疼痛，这些部位摸起来可能会感到温暖并肿胀。所有这些症状都是免疫反应的迹象。这些反应非常普遍，通常表明免疫系统正在努力识别疫苗并下次记住新病毒。
  在有执照的医疗提供者的监督下接种疫苗始终很重要。预约有疫苗的指定诊所或零售药房。与您的医生核对是否有任何担忧，因为大多数地方都有明确的疫苗分配计划。

note_en:
As part of President Biden’s push to get at least 100 million COVID-19 vaccine doses into the arms of people in the U.S. by the end of April, the White House announced February 9 it will begin shipping doses to 1,300 federally qualified community health centers. These organizations primarily serve patients from Black and brown communities who fall below the poverty line. On Tuesday the administration announced plans to double the number of vaccines it is sending to retail pharmacies to two million doses. The efforts add to existing distribution to hospitals, state and municipal agencies. But the lack of informed messaging from the Trump administration, combined with the range of different COVID vaccines, the emergence of new coronavirus variants, and inconsistent state and municipal rollout plans, have caused confusion and driven vaccine hesitancy.  Scientific American asked Namandjé Bumpus, a pharmacologist at Johns Hopkins Medicine in Baltimore, and Ashley Lauren St. John, an immunologist at Duke-NUS Medical School in Singapore to answer some of the biggest questions about the currently available COVID vaccines.
   Yes! If you are currently eligible to get a vaccine, go ahead and get it. We really need more people to get vaccinated to build herd immunity and slow the spread of disease. Vaccines are an important part of our toolkit for moving us out of the pandemic. If you have individual reasons for hesitation, talk to your health care provider.
  The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has not suggested a minimum interval between recovering from COVID and getting vaccinated. Your symptoms should be gone, and you should be released from quarantine before you go get a vaccine so as not to give COVID to anyone else. It may be true that you can wait longer because you have some immune response built up—talk to your health care provider if you think that applies to you. [ Editor’s Note: In contrast with most public health guidance, White House chief medical adviser Anthony Fauci said he believes people who have been infected should   wait 90 days before getting vaccinated .]
 How should you interpret vaccine efficacy—what does “95 percent efficacy” or “66 percent efficacy” even mean?
 Vaccine  efficacy  refers to how well it performs in a carefully controlled trial, whereas  effectiveness describes its performance in the real world. A common mistake is to interpret a 95 percent efficacy to mean you have a 5 percent chance of getting sick even if you get vaccinated. That is not true at all. Efficacy is calculated based on trials that have an unvaccinated placebo control group, and at the end of the trial, they look at the number in the control group that ended up with symptomatic COVID to get the baseline infection rate. The Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine, for instance, had 95 percent efficacy in its clinical trial. That number came from the fact that 162 people in the placebo group got symptomatic COVID, and eight people in the vaccinated group did; that is  170 cases total. Eight is approximately five percent of 170. That is essentially how efficacy is calculated. Most COVID vaccine clinical trials have reported efficacy in preventing any symptomatic disease, not necessarily severe disease; early data for the latter are promising, however.
 If you get the vaccine and still get infected, does the vaccine still make a difference? Will it prevent severe disease or death?
 Yes, what we’ve seen with most of the current vaccines is less severe symptoms and almost no hospitalizations and deaths in vaccinated people who become infected. Several vaccine trials have observed a reduction in severe disease in the vaccinated subjects. With a few exceptions—such as, possibly, the AstraZeneca vaccine in South Africa—the vaccines appear to reduce your risk of getting symptomatic COVID, but if you do get it, it’s very likely to be a less severe case.
 Do the vaccines protect against the new virus variants, including those first identified in the U.K., South Africa and Brazil?
 The data so far suggest that most of the vaccines do provide at least some protection against the new variants. We can see from some of the clinical trial data and antibody neutralization studies, however, that vaccines designed against the original strains of the virus might not be quite as effective against certain new variants—particularly against mutations found in the B.1.351 variant that is now widespread in South Africa. Many of the new variants have changes in the spike protein, which the virus uses to infect cells, so it’s a little bit different than what a vaccinated person’s body has seen before in the vaccine. Still, overall, the vaccines authorized in the U.S. are meeting efficacy targets and seem to give some protection against the new variants. The best way to prevent new variants from emerging is by getting lots of people vaccinated. Moderna is developing a booster shot to offer additional protection if needed, and Pfizer-BioNTech  is exploring this possibility as well.
   An mRNA vaccine—such as the ones made by Pfizer-BioNTech or Moderna—provides genetic instructions for making viral proteins in the form of single-stranded RNA, in a lipid coat, to your cells, without injecting any virus itself. This has some exciting advantages because it only contains those virus components that you want the immune system to recognize, and it causes your own cells to mimic the way viral proteins are made when a cell is naturally infected with the virus. It is remarkably good at inducing the same type of immune response that would occur in a natural infection. A downside is that the mRNA needs to be kept very cold for it to be stable.
 Viral vector vaccines—such as those made by AstraZeneca or Johnson & Johnson—use double-stranded DNA instead of RNA, but are still delivering genetic instructions to your cells. They use a modified, safe virus (not SARS-CoV-2) to deliver the instructions for making viral proteins.
  Different vaccine designs are chosen because they have unique advantages, so there is not a “best” vaccine, but there might be a vaccine that is better for a certain group of people to take. Both mRNA and viral vector vaccines instruct your cells to make pieces of the coronavirus spike protein so that the next time your body encounters those proteins it can mount an immune response. Efficacy numbers are derived from different clinical studies that were done in different settings, so instead of comparing those numbers, the thing to take away is that the authorized vaccines generally work. They have efficacy.
  We hope so, but we don’t fully know yet. There are two key goals of vaccines: to protect the individual and to stop the spread of disease. We do have reason to be hopeful based on what we know about viruses and some  emerging data, but we still don’t know for sure  if vaccinations will reduce disease transmission. That is why it’s important to continue social distancing, wearing masks and following other guidelines to prevent the spread of disease, even if you have been vaccinated.
  This could happen. Vaccines often need booster doses to confer the best levels of protection. Some vaccines can protect someone for a lifetime whereas others require a boost every few years. It will be important to monitor immune responses over time in those who are vaccinated so we will know if and when boosters are needed. Most of the currently available COVID vaccines  require two doses to be fully effective. The CDC  recently expanded the recommended allowable interval between doses because of limited availability, although some experts have  criticized the decision.
  Allergic reactions can occur in response to any injected vaccines or drugs, but they are extremely rare. The vaccines should always be administered in a setting where health care providers can treat any allergic reactions that develop unexpectedly. People don’t need to be concerned about the vaccines if they have food allergies or latex allergies. Most people don’t need to worry about an allergic reaction. For those with a history of severe allergic reactions or allergies to the vaccine ingredients, it would be best to discuss it with a doctor before getting the COVID vaccine.
  The common side effects are what we would expect for other vaccines: fatigue, headache, fever—particularly after the second dose—and acute pain at the injection site, which can feel warm to the touch and swollen; all of these symptoms are signs of immune reaction. These reactions are very common and often indicate that the immune system is working hard to recognize the vaccine and remember the new virus for next time.
  It is always important to take vaccines under the supervision of a licensed medical provider. Make an appointment with a designated clinic or retail pharmacy where vaccines are available. Check with your doctor about any concerns because there are clear distribution plans for vaccines in most places.

 第一和第二剂之间的间隔时间越长，会使COVID疫苗更有效吗？该结果是可能的。不是所有的COVID疫苗都是一样的，最佳的给药时间表取决于具体的设计。一些疫苗基于称为mRNA的易碎遗传物质条带，一些则依赖于更坚硬的DNA，而另一些则使用蛋白质片段。这些核心可以带入包裹在微小脂质滴或无害黑猩猩病毒中的细胞中。
 鉴于这种差异，丹尼对基于DNA的牛津-阿斯利康疫苗进行了测试并在两次注射间隔12周后发现有效就不足为奇了。这比基于mRNA的Moderna和Pfizer疫苗的建议间隔时间长约三到四倍。随着时间的推移，研究人员可能会发现与最初的临床试验中所测试的给药方案稍有不同的给药方案更为有效。丹尼说：“您本来可以进行两年的剂量研究，但这在像这样的世界中并不是最负责任的事情。” “不要让完美成为善良的敌人。”
